# dynamoip-examples

> Docker examples for [dynamoip](https://github.com/foundanand/dynamoip) — run dynamoip and your services together in containers, with trusted HTTPS reachable from any device on the network.

This repo contains ready-to-run Docker Compose examples. Each example is a self-contained directory with its own `docker-compose.yml`, `dynamoip.config.json`, `.env.example`, and a `setup-env.js` script.

---

## How Docker examples work

dynamoip proxies HTTPS traffic to local services by `Host` header, always targeting `localhost:PORT`. In Docker, each container has its own network namespace, so `localhost` inside one container does not reach another container.

**The solution used in these examples: socat port forwarding inside the dynamoip container.**

A shell entrypoint (`entrypoint.sh`) reads `FORWARD_<service>=<port>` environment variables and starts a `socat` forwarder for each one:

```sh
socat TCP-LISTEN:<port>,fork,reuseaddr TCP:<service>:<port> &
```

This bridges `localhost:PORT` inside the dynamoip container to the Docker service name over the internal Docker network. dynamoip never knows it isn't talking to a local process.

---

## LAN IP and Docker Desktop (macOS/Windows)

On Linux, `os.networkInterfaces()` returns the real LAN IP. On macOS and Windows, Docker Desktop runs containers inside a Linux VM — `os.networkInterfaces()` returns the VM's internal IP (e.g. `172.17.x.x`), which is not reachable from other devices on the LAN.

**Solution:** set `LAN_IP` in `.env` to the host machine's real LAN IP. dynamoip checks `process.env.LAN_IP` first and skips auto-detection when it is set.

Each example includes `setup-env.js` which detects the LAN IP using the OS-native command and writes it to `.env`:
- macOS: `ipconfig getifaddr en0` (falls back to en1, en2)
- Linux: `hostname -I`
- Windows: PowerShell `Get-NetIPAddress` filtered to DHCP-assigned IPv4

If detection fails, the script exits with a clear error — do not proceed to `docker compose up` until it succeeds.

Docker Compose enforces this with:
```yaml
LAN_IP: "${LAN_IP:?LAN_IP is not set. Run: node setup-env.js}"
```

---

## Port binding on macOS/Windows

`network_mode: host` does NOT work on Docker Desktop for macOS or Windows. It shares the Linux VM's network namespace, not the host machine's. Ports 80 and 443 are exposed via explicit `ports:` mappings instead:

```yaml
ports:
  - "80:80"
  - "443:443"
```

Docker Desktop proxies these from the Mac/Windows host into the VM, so dynamoip's proxy is reachable from the LAN.

On Linux, `network_mode: host` is an alternative that works correctly.

---

## Typical example structure

```
<example-name>/
├── setup-env.js              Run first — detects LAN IP, creates .env
├── docker-compose.yml        All services + dynamoip
├── dynamoip.config.json      baseDomain + domain→port mapping
├── .env.example              CF_API_TOKEN, CF_EMAIL, LAN_IP template
├── .gitignore                Excludes .env
├── README.md                 Setup instructions
├── <service-a>/
│   ├── Dockerfile            Single-stage (no deps) or multi-stage (has deps)
│   ├── server.js
│   └── index.html
├── <service-b>/
│   └── ...
└── dynamoip/
    ├── Dockerfile            Multi-stage: deps stage installs dynamoip@latest, runner stage copies node_modules
    └── entrypoint.sh         Starts socat forwarders then exec's dynamoip
```

---

## Dockerfile conventions

**Services with no npm dependencies** (plain Node.js, no `node_modules`): use a single-stage Dockerfile.

```dockerfile
FROM node:22-alpine
RUN addgroup -S app && adduser -S app -G app
WORKDIR /app
COPY server.js index.html ./
RUN chown -R app:app /app
USER app
EXPOSE <port>
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

**Services with npm dependencies**: use multi-stage. Stage 1 installs deps, stage 2 copies only `node_modules` and source into a clean image. This keeps build tools and npm cache out of the final image.

**dynamoip container**: always multi-stage. Stage 1 runs `npm install dynamoip@latest`. Stage 2 copies `node_modules` into a clean alpine image, adds `socat`, and uses `entrypoint.sh` as the entrypoint.

```dockerfile
FROM node:22-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
RUN npm install dynamoip@latest

FROM node:22-alpine AS runner
RUN apk add --no-cache socat
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY entrypoint.sh ./
RUN chmod +x entrypoint.sh
ENTRYPOINT ["./entrypoint.sh"]
```

Runs as root — required to bind to ports 80 and 443.

---

## docker-compose.yml conventions

- All app services expose only internal ports (no `ports:` needed — dynamoip proxies to them via socat)
- All app services have a `healthcheck` so dynamoip waits for them before starting
- The dynamoip service has `ports: ["80:80", "443:443"]`
- The dynamoip service uses `env_file: .env` and passes `FORWARD_<service>=<port>` env vars
- A named volume (`dynamoip-certs`) persists the Let's Encrypt certificate across restarts
- `restart: unless-stopped` on all services

```yaml
services:
  myapp:
    build: ./myapp
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-qO-", "http://localhost:<port>"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 5s

  dynamoip:
    build: ./dynamoip
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    restart: unless-stopped
    env_file: .env
    environment:
      LAN_IP: "${LAN_IP:?LAN_IP is not set. Run: node setup-env.js}"
      FORWARD_myapp: <port>
    volumes:
      - dynamoip-certs:/root/.localmap/certs
      - ./dynamoip.config.json:/config/dynamoip.config.json:ro
    depends_on:
      myapp:
        condition: service_healthy

volumes:
  dynamoip-certs:
```

---

## dynamoip.config.json conventions

Use `yourdomain.com` as the placeholder `baseDomain` in committed files. Users replace this with their own domain.

```json
{
  "baseDomain": "yourdomain.com",
  "domains": {
    "myapp": 3000
  }
}
```

The domain names in `domains` must match the `FORWARD_<name>` env vars and the Docker service names in `docker-compose.yml`.

---

## .env.example conventions

```env
# Cloudflare credentials (required for Pro mode)
CF_API_TOKEN=your_cloudflare_api_token_here
CF_EMAIL=you@example.com

# Required on macOS and Windows — set to your machine's LAN IP (e.g. 192.168.1.42)
# On Linux, leave unset — dynamoip auto-detects it.
# LAN_IP=
```

Never commit `.env`. Always commit `.env.example`. Always include `.env` in `.gitignore`.

---

## Running an example

```bash
cd <example-name>
node setup-env.js        # detects LAN IP → writes .env
# edit .env: fill in CF_API_TOKEN and CF_EMAIL
docker compose up --build
```

To stop:
```bash
docker compose down
```

To also remove the cert volume (forces re-issue on next start):
```bash
docker compose down -v
```

---

## What not to do

- Do not use `network_mode: host` in examples — it only works on Linux and silently fails on macOS/Windows
- Do not hardcode a real domain in `dynamoip.config.json` — use `yourdomain.com`
- Do not commit `.env` — it contains real Cloudflare credentials
- Do not add `ports:` to app service containers — they are internal; dynamoip reaches them via socat
- Do not run `docker compose up` before `node setup-env.js` — `LAN_IP` will be missing and Compose will refuse to start
- Do not use multi-stage builds for services with no npm dependencies — it breaks because `node_modules` is never created
- Do not use `version:` at the top of `docker-compose.yml` — it is obsolete in modern Docker Compose
